risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "After completion of baseline assessments, participants were randomized to one of two treatment groups by blocked randomization with stratification on gender. The random allocation sequence was provided in sealed envelopes by the study statistician (who had no contact with participants) to a staff member not involved in data collection." Comment: This describes a systematic random allocation procedure using blocked randomization with stratification, with the sequence generated/provided by the study statistician. These are clear context clues indicating a proper random sequence generation method, so Low risk is appropriate.

_evidence.method_quote: After completion of baseline assessments, participants were randomized to one of two treatment groups by blocked randomization with stratification on gender. The random allocation sequence was provided in sealed envelopes by the study statistician (who had no contact with participants) to a staff member not involved in data collection.
_evidence.method_summary: Participants were randomized using blocked randomization stratified by gender, with the sequence placed in sealed envelopes by the study statistician.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The random allocation sequence was provided in sealed envelopes by the study statistician (who had no contact with participants) to a staff member not involved in data collection." Comment: This explicitly describes a concealment process using sealed envelopes and separates the statistician from participant contact, indicating the recruiter could not foresee the next assignment before enrollment.

_evidence.concealment_quote: After completion of baseline assessments, participants were randomized to one of two treatment groups by blocked randomization with stratification on gender. The random allocation sequence was provided in sealed envelopes by the study statistician (who had no contact with participants) to a staff member not involved in data collection.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The trial was technically single-blinded given that patients were aware of which intervention they were assigned to (CRM or HE). However, participants were masked to the research hypothesis and both interventions were presented to participants as health promoting; this design should minimize expectation bias." Comment: Participants knew their assigned intervention, so blinding of participants was absent/incomplete. Because the study assessed multiple subjective outcomes (e.g. depressive symptoms, anxiety, perceived stress, anger, hostility, physical activity), knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence outcomes, so the dominant performance-bias risk is High.

_evidence.blinding_quote: The trial was technically single-blinded given that patients were aware of which intervention they were assigned to (CRM or HE). However, participants were masked to the research hypothesis and both interventions were presented to participants as health promoting; this design should minimize expectation bias. Investigators and the staff collecting the data were blinded to the treatment status of the participants. The two treatment providers were not involved with participant recruitment, data collection, analysis or interpretation.
_evidence.blinding_method: participants were masked to the research hypothesis; both interventions were presented to participants as health promoting

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Investigators and the staff collecting the data were blinded to the treatment status of the participants. ... All outcome data were collected in a blinded fashion with respect to the participants' treatment status." Comment: The outcome assessors/staff collecting data were explicitly blinded. The study also used blinded assessment for subjective/self-reported psychosocial outcomes and objective laboratory/vascular measures, so knowledge of allocation was unlikely to bias outcome measurement.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: Investigators and the staff collecting the data were blinded to the treatment status of the participants. ... All outcome data were collected in a blinded fashion with respect to the participants' treatment status.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Of the randomized patients, 21 and 23 participants in the CRM and HE groups, respectively, attended the 6-month visit and 19 in each group attended the 12-month visit." Comment: The primary outcome (FMD) was assessed at 12 months, and by that time 14/33 (42%) of CRM and 16/35 (46%) of HE were not observed at the visit, with a differential loss of 11.7 percentage points (33 randomized vs 19 analysed in CRM; 35 randomized vs 19 analysed in HE). Although the authors used ITT/repeated-measures mixed effects models, the observed attrition is substantial and imbalanced at the analysed time point, meeting the high-risk threshold for incomplete outcome data.

per_arm_flow.reasons: Of the randomized patients, 21 ... attended the 6-month visit and 19 in each group attended the 12-month visit. Biomarker data were available for 29 participants randomized to CRM (83%).
Of the randomized patients, 23 ... attended the 6-month visit and 19 in each group attended the 12-month visit. Biomarker data were available for 27 participants randomized to HE (82%).

_evidence.missing_data_method: intention-to-treat; repeated measures mixed effects models
_evidence.notes: Analysed numbers are explicitly reported for biomarker data only (29 CRM, 27 HE); the article also states the primary and secondary outcomes were analysed under ITT with all available randomized participants (n=68) using mixed effects models, but per-arm analysed numbers for the primary outcome are not given.
